The lesson I took away was that build vs buy isn't binary — the best option is often 'buy the core, build the differentiation.'
pitfalls: Don't just pick one without showing the analysis
Don't describe a decision where there was obviously only one choice
Don't ignore maintenance cost in your evaluation

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult feedback or bad news.
structure: Context: what was the bad news and to whom
How you prepared (timing, setting, framing)
What you said (be specific about language)
How the recipient responded
Follow-up and outcome
prompts.sample_answer: I had to tell a senior engineer on my team that he wasn't being promoted this cycle. I'm glad you asked that, because it's one of the hardest conversations I've had. I prepared by writing down specific, behavioral feedback — not 'you're not ready' but 'in the last 3 projects, the code reviews identified these patterns that indicate Staff-level behavior isn't consistently demonstrated yet.' I framed it as 'here's exactly what needs to change, and here's my plan to help you get there.' He was upset initially, but the specificity gave him something to work on. Six months later, he was promoted. What I'd really highlight is that vague feedback is cruel — specific feedback is a gift, even when it hurts.
pitfalls: Don't describe easy feedback (telling someone about a minor bug)
Don't be vague about what you said — quote yourself
Don't skip the follow-up — delivery isn't the end

prompts.text: Tell me about a process you changed that made your team more effective.
patterns: The lesson I took away was...
structure: What was the existing process and its problems
How you identified the root cause (not just the symptom)
What you changed and why
Measurable improvement
How you got team buy-in
prompts.sample_answer: Our deployment process required 3 manual approval steps and took 45 minutes per deploy. Developers deployed once a week because it was so painful. Before this... After this... Before: 3 manual approvals, 45 minutes, weekly deploys. After: automated canary deploys with rollback triggers, 5 minutes, multiple deploys per day. This resulted in deployment frequency increasing 10x and rollback time dropping from 30 minutes to 90 seconds. The lesson I took away was that the best process improvements remove steps rather than add them, and the way to get buy-in is to make the new process so much easier that people switch voluntarily.
pitfalls: Don't describe a trivial process change (changing a meeting time)
Don't skip the before/after numbers
Don't add process — describe removing or simplifying it

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you had to coordinate work across multiple teams.
structure: Context: what initiative, how many teams, what was at stake
The coordination challenge (dependencies, misalignment)
Your approach to alignment (documents, meetings, shared goals)
How you handled the hardest dependency
Outcome + what you learned about cross-team work
prompts.sample_answer: We were migrating from a monolith to microservices and needed to coordinate changes across the platform team, the payments team, and the mobile team. The other piece was that each team had different release schedules. I wrote a migration contract — a shared document specifying exactly what each team would change, in what order, and what the integration test would verify. Building on that, I set up a weekly 15-minute sync where each team reported their status against the contract. This resulted in the migration completing 2 weeks ahead of schedule with zero production incidents. The lesson I took away was that cross-team coordination works when the contract is explicit and the sync is lightweight.
pitfalls: Don't describe a single-team project
Don't skip the hardest dependency
Don't say 'I just communicated a lot' — show the structure

prompts.text: Tell me about a technical decision you made that turned out to be wrong.
patterns: The lesson I took away was...
structure: The decision and your reasoning at the time
How you discovered it was wrong
What you did to fix it
The systemic change you made so it wouldn't recur
What you learned about decision-making
prompts.sample_answer: I chose MongoDB for our transactional system because the schema was evolving rapidly. Six months later, we had data consistency issues that were causing real customer impact. Looking back, I would have started with PostgreSQL and used JSON columns for the evolving parts — we'd have gotten schema flexibility plus ACID guarantees. When I realized the mistake, I led the migration back to PostgreSQL. The lesson I took away was that 'schema flexibility' is not worth sacrificing consistency for transactional data. I also added a decision template to our architecture review process that forces us to write down: what happens if we're wrong, and how hard is it to reverse?
pitfalls: Don't pick a decision where you were actually right in hindsight
Don't skip the systemic change — individual learning isn't enough
Don't blame the technology — own your decision

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you went beyond the requirements to solve a real customer problem.
patterns: To give you a concrete example...
structure: The stated requirements vs the real customer need
How you discovered the deeper need
What you did beyond the spec
Customer reaction and measurable impact
Principle about customer-centricity
prompts.sample_answer: The product spec said 'add a CSV export button.' What clicked for me was that the support tickets weren't about exporting — they were about spending 4 hours every Friday manually reformatting the exported data into their reporting format. To give you a concrete example: one customer exported 50K rows and then spent 3 hours in Excel doing vlookups. I built a customizable export template that let users define their own column mapping and filters. This resulted in a 95% reduction in support tickets about exports and the feature became the #2 reason for upgrades. The lesson I took away was that requirements describe symptoms — you have to understand the job the customer is trying to do.
pitfalls: Don't describe gold-plating (adding features nobody asked for)
Don't skip how you discovered the real need
Don't ignore the spec entirely — show you addressed it AND went beyond

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you had to redesign something to handle significant growth.
patterns: At our scale, we had to...
structure: What was the system and how it was originally designed
The growth signal that triggered the redesign
Your analysis of the bottleneck
The new design and key decisions
Results at the new scale
prompts.sample_answer: Our notification service was a single Postgres instance with a polling model. Before this... After this... Before: single DB, 100 notifications/sec, 2-second latency. After: sharded Kafka + Redis with push model, 50K notifications/sec, 50ms latency. At our scale, we had to move from polling to pushing because the DB couldn't handle 10K concurrent polls. The trade-off here is between simplicity (polling) and efficiency (push with the complexity of WebSocket management). This resulted in handling 500x the traffic with lower latency. The lesson I took away was that scaling redesigns should be data-driven — we measured before we decided what to change.
pitfalls: Don't describe premature optimization — show real growth pressure
Don't skip the bottleneck analysis
Don't forget to name the trade-offs in your new design

prompts.text: Tell me about a time security requirements conflicted with delivery timelines.
structure: The security requirement and the delivery pressure
Your analysis of the risk (probabilistic, not absolute)
How you balanced the trade-off
The compromise or creative solution
Outcome and principle
prompts.sample_answer: Security required all API calls to use mTLS, but implementing it would delay our launch by 3 weeks. I'd argue that the trade-off here is between perfect security and actual security. Shipping without any auth is dangerous, but a 3-week delay means 3 weeks with zero customers getting value. I proposed: ship with API key auth (sufficient for launch), add mTLS in the next sprint (2 weeks post-launch), and put all internal services behind a VPN in the meantime. This resulted in launching on time, zero security incidents during the 2-week window, and mTLS shipping 2 weeks later. The lesson I took away was that risk management is about reducing risk to acceptable levels, not eliminating it — and a shipped product with good-enough security beats an unshipped product with perfect security.
pitfalls: Don't describe a situation where security was clearly non-negotiable (e.g., PCI compliance)
Don't dismiss security concerns — show you took them seriously
Don't skip the compromise — they want to see creative problem-solving

prompts.text: Tell me about something you automated that had a big impact.
patterns: To give you a concrete example...
structure: What was manual and how painful it was
How you identified it as an automation target
What you built and the key design decisions
Before/after with numbers
How you got people to trust and use the automation
prompts.sample_answer: To give you a concrete example — one BA spent two days a week compiling data from five different systems into a single report. Before this... After this... Before: 16 hours/week of manual work, errors in 15% of reports, delivered Friday afternoon. After: automated pipeline runs in 30 minutes, zero errors, delivered Monday 6 AM. I built it as a scheduled Lambda that pulls from each system, transforms, and writes to a shared spreadsheet. This resulted in saving 800 person-hours/year and the BA moved to strategic analysis work. The lesson I took away was that the best automation targets are high-frequency, error-prone manual processes that trap smart people in repetitive work.
pitfalls: Don't describe automating something trivial (a one-off script)
Don't skip the before/after numbers
Don't forget the trust angle — people don't use automation they don't trust

prompts.text: Tell me about a time the team was split on a technical decision and how you resolved it.
patterns: I see it a bit differently...
structure: The decision and the two camps
Why each side had valid concerns
How you facilitated resolution (data, prototype, compromise)
The decision and how you got buy-in from the other side
Outcome and principle
prompts.sample_answer: Half the team wanted GraphQL and half wanted REST for our new API. I see it a bit differently — both have valid trade-offs. The trade-off here is flexibility vs simplicity. Instead of debating, I proposed: build a REST API for the 5 most common operations (covering 90% of traffic), and add a GraphQL endpoint for the complex query patterns that the mobile team needed. I built a prototype of both in a week and we measured: REST was 3x faster for simple queries, GraphQL was 2x better for complex ones. The data resolved the debate. This resulted in shipping both and the team moved on in a week instead of debating for a month. The lesson I took away was that data beats debate — and most technical disagreements have a 'both' solution if you look for it.
pitfalls: Don't describe a situation where one side was obviously wrong
Don't say 'I just decided' — show the facilitation
Don't skip how you got the losing side to buy in

prompts.text: Why do you want to work here specifically?
patterns: What I'm really looking for is...
structure: Specific technical or cultural attraction (not generic)
How it connects to your career trajectory
What you'd contribute (not just what you'd get)
Evidence you've researched the company
prompts.sample_answer: What I'm really looking for is a team where platform engineering is a first-class discipline, not an afterthought. What clicked for me was reading your engineering blog about the internal developer platform — the fact that you measure developer productivity as a first-class metric tells me you take platform work seriously. Over time I became the platform engineering advocate at my current company, and I want to join a place where I can learn from people who've been doing it longer and at larger scale. Specifically, I'd want to contribute to your deployment orchestration work — my experience with canary deployments would be directly applicable.
pitfalls: Don't give a generic answer ('great culture, smart people')
Don't focus only on what you'll get — show what you'll contribute
Don't say things that could apply to any company

prompts.text: Design a URL shortener like Bitly.
structure: Requirements: short URL creation, redirect, analytics, high read ratio
Estimation: 100M URLs/day, 10:1 read:write, ~2TB storage
Storage: key-value store (short_code → long_url + metadata)
High-level: API server, KV store, cache, analytics pipeline
API: POST /shorten, GET /{code} → 301/302
Detail: ID generation — auto-increment + base62 vs random key
Trade-offs: 301 (cached, fast) vs 302 (trackable, slower)
prompts.sample_answer: At a high level, there are three components: the API layer, the storage layer, and the analytics pipeline. The trade-off here is between 301 and 302 redirects — 301 lets browsers cache, which is faster but means we can't track clicks. I'd use 302 with a caching layer for the hot paths. For ID generation, the simpler approach would be to use a pre-generated pool of random keys stored in a database — this avoids coordination and we can generate them in batches offline. For storage, any KV store works — DynamoDB or Cassandra for scale. Cache the top 1% of URLs in Redis (the viral ones). Analytics go to a Kafka topic and get aggregated downstream.
pitfalls: Don't skip estimation — they want to see you can reason about scale
Don't over-engineer the ID generation — start simple
Don't forget about the analytics requirement

prompts.text: Design a real-time chat system like WhatsApp.
patterns: The failure mode I'm most concerned about is...
structure: Requirements: 1:1 and group chat, online status, message history
Estimation: 50M DAU, 10K msg/s peak
Storage: message table partitioned by conversation_id
High-level: WebSocket servers, message queue, presence service
Detail: fan-out on write vs read for group chats
Trade-offs: WebSocket vs long polling, ordering guarantees
prompts.sample_answer: At a high level, we have connection handlers, a message routing layer, and storage. The trade-off here is fan-out strategy: for 1:1 chat, write to both users' inboxes. For group chat with <50 members, fan-out on write. For larger groups, fan-out on read. The failure mode I'm most concerned about is message ordering — if two messages arrive at different servers simultaneously, we need to preserve order within a conversation. I'd use per-conversation sequence numbers generated by a single partition owner. For presence, use heartbeats with TTL in Redis — simpler than a dedicated presence service.
pitfalls: Don't forget offline message delivery
Don't ignore the group chat fan-out problem
Don't skip message ordering guarantees

prompts.text: Design a social news feed like Twitter.
patterns: If we zoom out for a moment...
structure: Requirements: personalized feed, ranking, pagination, real-time
Estimation: 100M DAU, 500M posts/day, avg 200 follows
Storage: post table, follow table, pre-computed feed table
Detail: hybrid fan-out (write for normal, read for celebrities)
Trade-offs: chronological vs ML ranking, pagination strategy
prompts.sample_answer: If we zoom out for a moment, the core problem is: given a user, return the N most relevant posts from people they follow, fast. The trade-off here is fan-out cost vs feed generation latency. I'd use a hybrid: when a normal user posts, push to all followers' pre-computed feeds (fan-out on write). When a celebrity posts, don't push — instead, merge their posts at read time. This gives O(1) feed reads for most users and O(celebrity_count) for celebrity followers. For ranking, start chronological and add ML ranking later. Use cursor-based pagination, not offset — new posts arriving during scroll would break offset-based pagination.
pitfalls: Don't ignore the celebrity problem (millions of followers)
Don't use offset pagination — it breaks with new posts
Don't skip the ranking discussion

prompts.text: Design a search autocomplete system.
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: Requirements: suggest as user types, <50ms latency, top 5
Estimation: 100M queries/day, top 5 per prefix
Storage: trie or N-gram with top-K completions per prefix
Detail: ranking by frequency + personalization + freshness
Trade-offs: trie size, update latency, personalization cost
prompts.sample_answer: The first thing I would do is clarify: are we suggesting queries or documents? Assuming queries. At a high level, we need a prefix index and a ranking function. The simpler approach would be to store top-K completions for each prefix — precompute the top 5 for every prefix up to length N. This uses more memory but gives O(1) lookup. For a query like 'how t', we look up the key 'how t' and return the precomputed list. Updates happen by incrementing a counter and periodically recomputing top-K. For trending queries, we can boost their score with a time-decay function. Shard by prefix first character for horizontal scaling.
pitfalls: Don't propose building a full trie traversal at query time — too slow
Don't forget about trending/fresh queries
Don't skip how you handle the cold-start problem for new prefixes

prompts.text: Design an object storage system like S3.
patterns: The failure mode I'm most concerned about is...
structure: Requirements: unlimited objects, 99.999999999% durability, 99.99% availability
Estimation: 100B objects, 1TB/day ingestion
Storage: erasure coding across nodes and zones
Detail: metadata service, data placement, garbage collection
Trade-offs: erasure coding vs replication, consistency model
prompts.sample_answer: The trade-off here is between replication and erasure coding. Erasure coding gives 11 nines durability with 1.4x storage overhead vs 3x for triple replication. I'd use erasure coding within a region (Reed-Solomon 8+4) and cross-region replication for disaster recovery. The failure mode I'm most concerned about is the metadata service becoming a bottleneck — every read and write hits it. We can mitigate that by partitioning metadata by object key prefix and caching hot metadata. For consistency: read-after-write for new objects (synchronous replication to quorum), eventual consistency for overwrites (async propagation). Garbage collection runs as a background sweep that reconciles reference counts.
pitfalls: Don't propose full replication — too expensive at S3 scale
Don't forget about the metadata bottleneck
Don't skip the garbage collection / orphan cleanup problem

prompts.text: Design a notification system supporting push, SMS, and email.
structure: Requirements: 3 channels, templates, preferences, delivery tracking
Estimation: 10M notifications/day, 100ms send latency
Storage: notification table, template store, preference store
Detail: priority queues, rate limiting, retry logic
Trade-offs: per-channel vs unified queue, retry vs idempotency
prompts.sample_answer: At a high level, there are four components: the API, the router, the channel workers, and the preference store. The simpler approach would be a single priority queue per channel — push workers read from the push queue, SMS from the SMS queue. Rate limiting uses a token bucket per user per channel, stored in Redis. We can mitigate double-sending by using idempotency keys — each notification gets a unique ID, and channel workers check if they've already sent it. For templates, store them in a document store and render at send time with user-specific variables. Track delivery status via callbacks from the channel providers (APNs delivery receipts, SNS delivery logs, SES bounce/complaint notifications).
pitfalls: Don't forget rate limiting — notification fatigue is real
Don't skip the preference/opt-out system
Don't ignore retry logic — but make it idempotent

prompts.text: Design a distributed web crawler.
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
The failure mode I'm most concerned about is...
structure: Requirements: discover, download, process, index billions of pages
Estimation: 10B pages, 1PB raw, 100 pages/s per node
Storage: URL frontier, raw HTML in object store, metadata in DB
Detail: deduplication, politeness, scheduling, distribution
Trade-offs: breadth vs depth, freshness vs coverage, politeness vs speed
prompts.sample_answer: The first thing I would do is define what we're crawling and how often. The trade-off here is breadth vs depth — do we crawl everything once, or focus on important sites frequently? I'd use a priority queue as the URL frontier, where priority is based on page importance and freshness requirements. Deduplication uses a Bloom filter — O(1) check for 'have we seen this URL?' with a small false positive rate. The failure mode I'm most concerned about is being impolite — hammering a small site with 100 requests/second. We mitigate with per-domain rate limiting: maintain a mapping of domain → last_crawl_time + min_interval. Distribute by partitioning the URL frontier by domain hash, so all URLs for one domain go to the same crawler node.
pitfalls: Don't forget the robots.txt / politeness requirement
Don't skip URL deduplication at scale
Don't ignore the freshness problem — when to re-crawl?

prompts.text: Design a distributed key-value store.
patterns: Under normal conditions... Under edge cases...
structure: Requirements: get/put/delete, highly available, low latency, tunable consistency
Estimation: 1TB data, 1M reads/s, 100K writes/s
Storage: consistent hashing, replication factor N
Detail: quorum reads/writes, vector clocks, read repair
Trade-offs: consistency level per operation, R+W>N guarantee
prompts.sample_answer: At a high level, data is partitioned across nodes using consistent hashing with virtual nodes, and each key is replicated to N nodes. The trade-off here is consistency vs latency — we let the client choose. For reads, specify R (how many replicas to read from). For writes, specify W (how many to acknowledge). If R+W>N, you get strong consistency. Under normal conditions, a write goes to the coordinator node, which sends it to all N replicas and waits for W acknowledgments. Under edge cases — a network partition — if W nodes are available, the write succeeds but some replicas are stale. We handle this with read repair: when a read hits multiple replicas with different versions, we reconcile using vector clocks and write the merged version back. Anti-entropy uses Merkle trees to efficiently compare replica states.
pitfalls: Don't skip the quorum read/write explanation
Don't forget conflict resolution (vector clocks or CRDTs)
Don't ignore the hot key / celebrity problem

prompts.text: Design a location-based service like Uber.
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: Requirements: find nearby drivers, real-time location updates, ETA
Estimation: 10M active users, updates every 5s, 1M queries/s
Storage: spatial index (geohash/quadtree/S2), driver location cache
Detail: geohashing, proximity search, location update pipeline
Trade-offs: index resolution vs query precision, update frequency vs write load
prompts.sample_answer: At a high level, we need to store driver locations and query for nearby drivers within a radius. The first thing I would do is choose a spatial indexing strategy. I'd use geohashing: encode lat/lng into a string where nearby locations share prefixes. To find drivers within 2km, compute the geohashes covering that area and query all matching cells. The trade-off here is resolution vs write load: 12-character geohashes give ~3cm precision but create many cells. I'd use 6-character (1.2km × 0.6km) and search the 9 surrounding cells for a 2km radius. Driver locations update every 5 seconds via a WebSocket connection. Store active driver locations in Redis (geohash → set of driver IDs) and use the GEOADD/GEORADIUS commands which handle this natively. For ETA estimation, call a routing API with the driver and rider coordinates.
pitfalls: Don't propose scanning all drivers — that's O(n)
Don't forget the write load from frequent location updates
Don't skip how you handle sparse vs dense areas

prompts.text: Design a video streaming platform like YouTube.
structure: Requirements: upload, transcode, stream, recommend, analytics
Estimation: 500M hours/day, 500 hours uploaded/minute
Storage: object storage for video, CDN for delivery, metadata DB
Detail: upload flow, transcoding pipeline, CDN strategy, recommendations
Trade-offs: transcoding latency vs quality, CDN cost vs coverage
prompts.sample_answer: At a high level, the pipeline is: upload → transcode → store → serve via CDN → recommend → track analytics. The trade-off here is between transcoding time and quality options. I'd transcode to 4 resolutions (1080p, 720p, 480p, 360p) using a distributed worker pool. Uploads go to object storage via presigned URLs — the client uploads directly, no bottleneck through our servers. CDN strategy: push popular content to edge locations, pull everything else on first view. We can mitigate the cold-start problem by proactively pushing newly uploaded videos from popular creators to edge nodes. For recommendations, use collaborative filtering pre-computed offline, served from a cache layer. Analytics events stream to a data warehouse via Kafka for both reporting and ML model retraining.
pitfalls: Don't forget adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH)
Don't skip the transcoding pipeline — it's the most expensive part
Don't ignore live streaming if relevant — very different from VOD

prompts.text: Walk me through how you'd handle a production outage.
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: Acknowledge and communicate (don't debug silently)
Mitigate first, root-cause later (reduce blast radius)
Form hypothesis and investigate systematically
Communicate status updates regularly
Post-mortem: root cause, timeline, action items
prompts.sample_answer: The first thing I would do is acknowledge the incident and page the on-call team. At a high level, my priority order is: 1) Mitigate — can we roll back, failover, or feature-flag off the affected path? 2) Communicate — post an incident status with what we know, even if it's just 'we're aware and investigating.' 3) Investigate — check the most likely causes first: recent deployments, downstream dependencies, infrastructure changes. I use a binary search approach: eliminate halves of the system until I find the failing component. After resolution, I write a post-mortem with: timeline, root cause, 5 Whys analysis, and specific action items with owners. This resulted in a culture where post-mortems are blameless and action items are tracked in the sprint.
pitfalls: Don't start debugging before mitigating — reduce blast radius first
Don't work alone — communicate early and often
Don't skip the post-mortem — the real value is preventing recurrence

prompts.text: How would you debug a sudden latency spike in your service?
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: Check dashboards: p50/p90/p99, error rate, throughput
Correlate with recent changes (deployments, config, load)
Narrow down: which endpoints, which users, which region
Check infrastructure: CPU, memory, disk, network
Check dependencies: downstream latency, cache hit rates, DB slow queries
prompts.sample_answer: The first thing I would do is look at the metrics dashboard to narrow the scope. Is it all endpoints or one? All regions or one? When did it start? Then I check: was there a recent deployment? A config change? A scheduled job running? If nothing obvious, the crux of it is usually one of three things: a slow downstream dependency (check circuit breaker logs), a cache miss spike (check cache hit rates and eviction rates), or a database problem (check slow query logs, connection pool usage, replication lag). I approach it systematically: eliminate possibilities starting with the most likely. Once I find the cause, I mitigate first (scale up, add caching, disable the slow feature) and then fix the root cause.
pitfalls: Don't guess — start with data and eliminate possibilities
Don't forget to check downstream services, not just your own
Don't skip the correlation with recent changes

prompts.text: How do you manage technical debt in a fast-moving team?
structure: Categorize: risk debt (security, reliability) vs friction debt (slow dev) vs design debt (wrong abstractions)
Prioritize: risk debt first, then friction that slows daily work
Allocate: 20% of sprint capacity consistently
Make it visible: track debt items in backlog with estimated cost
Prevent: code review standards, architecture decision records
prompts.sample_answer: My approach to technical debt is to treat it like credit card debt — it's fine to carry a balance, but you need a paydown plan and you need to understand the interest rate. The trade-off here is between shipping now and paying later. I categorize debt into three buckets: risk debt (it might break — security vulnerabilities, missing tests for critical paths), friction debt (it slows us down — slow builds, flaky tests, manual deployments), and design debt (it's hard to change — wrong abstractions, tight coupling). Risk debt gets fixed immediately. Friction debt gets 20% of every sprint. Design debt gets addressed during feature work that touches that code. This resulted in our build time going from 12 minutes to 3 minutes and our deployment frequency increasing 10x over 6 months.
pitfalls: Don't say 'I don't let technical debt accumulate' — unrealistic
Don't ignore the difference between risk debt and convenience debt
Don't skip how you make debt visible to stakeholders

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you had to work with a difficult legacy codebase.
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: How you approached understanding the codebase
What made it difficult (technical and social)
Your strategy for making changes safely
Impact of your work
What you learned about working with legacy systems
prompts.sample_answer: I inherited a 10-year-old Java service with no tests, no documentation, and the original authors had left. The first thing I would do is characterize the risk: which parts are stable (don't touch) and which are fragile (need tests before changes). I added characterization tests — tests that capture current behavior, even if it's buggy — for the critical paths. Then I used the strangler fig pattern: built new features as separate services that called the legacy system, gradually routing traffic away. Before this... After this... Before, every change took 2 weeks and broke something. After, new features shipped in days without touching the legacy code. Looking back, I would have started with better observability — you can't safely change what you can't see.
pitfalls: Don't describe a rewrite — they want to see incremental approach
Don't bash the legacy code or its authors
Don't skip the testing strategy — that's how you change safely

prompts.text: Tell me about your most challenging on-call experience.
patterns: The lesson I took away was...
structure: Hook: the alert and initial severity
Your immediate actions (triage, communication)
The investigation and resolution
Systemic improvements you drove afterward
What you learned about operational excellence
prompts.sample_answer: I got paged at 3 AM: error rate on the payment service went from 0.1% to 40%. What surprised me was that the dashboard showed the problem started 2 hours before the alert triggered — our alerting threshold was too high. I immediately checked for recent deployments — one had gone out 3 hours before. I rolled back and the error rate dropped to normal within 5 minutes. This resulted in three systemic changes: 1) lowered the alerting threshold to catch degradation earlier, 2) added automatic rollback triggers that revert a deployment if error rate exceeds 5% within the first 30 minutes, 3) added a deployment checklist that requires monitoring the service for 30 minutes post-deploy. The lesson I took away was that every incident is a systems problem, not a people problem — if an incident can happen, the system should prevent it or detect it faster.
pitfalls: Don't describe a trivial page (disk space warning)
Don't skip the systemic improvements — they want to see you prevent recurrence
Don't work alone in the story — show communication

prompts.text: What's your philosophy on code reviews?
patterns: The key thing to understand is...
structure: Core principle (teaching vs gatekeeping, speed vs thoroughness)
How you prioritize review comments (blocking vs suggestions)
How you handle disagreements
How you ensure timely reviews
Measurable impact of your approach
prompts.sample_answer: My approach to code review is to understand the intent before evaluating the implementation. The key thing to understand is that a code review is a conversation, not an exam. I categorize my comments into three types: blocking (must fix — bugs, security, breaking changes), important (should fix — performance, readability), and suggestions (optional — style, alternative approaches). Blocking comments prevent merge. Everything else is a discussion. I aim to review within 4 hours — stale reviews waste everyone's time. This resulted in our team's review turnaround dropping from 2 days to 4 hours, and developer satisfaction with the review process going from 3/5 to 4.5/5 in surveys. The lesson I took away was that review speed matters more than review thoroughness — a fast review with 80% coverage beats a slow review with 100%.
pitfalls: Don't be a gatekeeper — show you enable, not block
Don't skip how you handle disagreements in reviews
Don't ignore the speed aspect — slow reviews kill productivity

prompts.text: How do you prioritize features when everything seems important?
patterns: The key thing to understand is...
structure: Your prioritization framework (impact/effort, RICE, etc.)
How you gather data (customer signals, metrics, stakeholder input)
How you communicate priorities (roadmap, backlog ranking)
How you handle pushback from stakeholders
Example of a specific prioritization decision
prompts.sample_answer: The key thing to understand is that everything is not equally important — stakeholders just have different perspectives on what matters most. The trade-off here is between being data-driven and being responsive to emerging needs. I use a simple framework: score each feature on customer impact (1-5), engineering effort (1-5), and strategic alignment (1-5). Customer impact is weighted 2x because building something nobody uses is the biggest waste. I also look at leading signals: support tickets, feature requests, usage analytics, competitive pressure. When stakeholders push back, I don't say no — I say 'yes, and here's where it fits in the queue.' This resulted in shipping the 3 features with the highest customer impact first, and they drove 40% more engagement than the features stakeholders originally prioritized.
pitfalls: Don't say 'I just do what the PM says' or 'I just pick the most interesting one'
Don't skip how you handle stakeholder pushback
Don't ignore customer signals — that's the whole point

prompts.text: Tell me about a time you had to convince your team to change direction.
structure: Context: what was the current direction and why change was needed
How you built the case (data, prototype, external evidence)
How you acknowledged the cost of changing
The turning point: what convinced the team
Outcome and what you learned about influence
prompts.sample_answer: The team was building a custom ORM because we thought it would be faster than adopting an existing one. I'd argue that maintaining a custom ORM will cost us 2+ hours per week per developer in bug fixes and feature additions, based on the commit history from the last 3 months. That said, I understand the hesitation — the existing ORMs don't handle our multi-tenant schema well. So I built a prototype showing that TypeORM with a custom decorator could handle our multi-tenant pattern in 50 lines of code. The turning point was when the most skeptical engineer reviewed the prototype and said 'this is actually cleaner than what we have.' This resulted in migrating in 2 weeks and saving 10+ hours/week of maintenance. The lesson I took away was that the best way to convince engineers is with code, not arguments.
pitfalls: Don't describe a situation where you had authority to just decide
Don't skip the data or evidence — opinions don't convince
Don't ignore the team's concerns — address them directly

prompts.text: Design a cross-border payment pipeline. How would you handle multi-currency transfers with FX conversion?
patterns: The failure mode I'm most concerned about is...
structure: Requirements: accept payments in 50+ currencies, real-time FX quotes, settlement in beneficiary currency, idempotent transfers
Estimation: 10K TPS peak (Black Friday), 99.99% availability, sub-2s end-to-end latency
High-level: Payment Gateway → FX Engine → Settlement Engine → Ledger → Bank Connector
Deep dive: Idempotency keys on every request, double-entry ledger for each currency, FX rate locking with TTL, async settlement with retry + dead-letter queue
Failure modes: Bank connector timeout → retry with idempotency; FX rate expired → re-quote; Ledger imbalance → reconciliation batch
prompts.sample_answer: I'd start by clarifying: are we handling both the payer and beneficiary side, or just one? Assuming both. The core flow is: client submits a transfer request with an idempotency key. The payment gateway validates and locks the FX rate for a short window, say 30 seconds. Then we debit the payer's account via a double-entry ledger entry. The settlement engine submits the payment to the beneficiary bank via SWIFT or local rails. If the bank confirms, we credit the beneficiary. If it fails, we reverse the debit. Every step writes to the append-only ledger for audit. For FX, we'd integrate with rate providers like FXCM or Reuters, cache rates in Redis with 1-second TTL, and lock the rate at quote time so the customer sees exactly what they'll pay.
pitfalls: Don't forget FX rate expiry — rates change every second
Double-entry must be per-currency — you can't debit USD and credit AUD in one entry
Settlement is async — don't block the API on bank confirmation
Reconciliation must run daily to catch silent bank failures
prompts.hint: Airwallex specifically cares about: idempotency, multi-currency ledger, and how you handle the gap between authorization and settlement.

prompts.text: Design a multi-currency ledger system. How do you ensure consistency across currency accounts?
patterns: Under normal conditions... Under edge cases...
structure: Requirements: multi-currency accounts, real-time balances, append-only audit trail, regulatory compliance
Estimation: 50K TPS, 7-year retention, sub-100ms balance queries
High-level: Ledger Service → PostgreSQL (sharded by account_id) → CDC → Balance Cache (Redis) → API

Deep dive: Each currency is a separate ledger account. Transfers create debit + credit entries in the same DB transaction. For cross-currency, three entries: debit USD, credit FX gain/loss, credit AUD. CQRS: write to Postgres, read from Redis cache updated via CDC.
Failure modes: Cross-shard transfer → two-phase commit or saga; Cache drift → read from DB for critical operations; GDPR erasure → pseudonymize, never delete ledger entries
prompts.sample_answer: A multi-currency ledger must guarantee that every transaction balances per currency. If I transfer 100 USD to AUD, I need: debit 100 USD from payer, credit 100 USD to FX account, debit equivalent AUD from FX account, credit AUD to beneficiary. All in one DB transaction on the same shard. I'd shard by a virtual account ID that groups all currency sub-accounts for one entity, so cross-currency transfers stay on one shard. For reads, I'd use CQRS: the ledger is the write model (PostgreSQL, ACID), and account balances are the read model (Redis, updated via CDC from Postgres). For the audit trail, I'd add a hash chain — each entry includes the SHA-256 of the previous entry — so tampering is detectable.
pitfalls: Never allow updates or deletes on ledger entries — reversing entries only
Cross-currency entries must still balance — track FX gain/loss explicitly
Don't put all currencies on one shard — group by entity, not currency
Cache is eventually consistent — for balance checks before transfers, read the DB
prompts.hint: Airwallex interviews often probe: what happens when a cross-currency transfer partially fails? How do you reconcile?

prompts.text: Design a real-time FX rate engine. How do you serve rates to thousands of clients with low latency?
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: Requirements: real-time FX rates for 100+ currency pairs, sub-50ms latency, rate locking for quotes
Estimation: 10K rate requests/s, 100K active quotes, rates update every 100ms from providers
High-level: Rate Provider Feed → Aggregator → Rate Cache (Redis) → Quote Service → Client

Deep dive: Subscribe to multiple FX providers via WebSocket. Aggregate by taking median or best rate. Store in Redis with currency-pair keys, 1-second TTL. Quote service locks rate for 30s by storing the locked rate in a separate Redis key with TTL. If rate expires, client must re-quote.

Failure modes: Provider feed drops → use last known rate with stale marker; Redis failure → fall back to DB with last persisted rates; Rate spike → circuit breaker to reject quotes that deviate >2% from recent average
prompts.sample_answer: The FX rate engine has two distinct problems: ingesting rates fast and serving them fast. For ingestion, I'd have a WebSocket connection to each FX provider. Rates come in every 100ms or so. I aggregate by taking the best bid/ask across providers, and push the updated rate to Redis with a 1-second TTL. For serving, the quote API reads from Redis, which gives sub-millisecond latency. When a client requests a quote for a transfer, I lock the rate by writing it to a Redis key like quote:{id} with a 30-second TTL. The payment has to be initiated within that window, or the rate expires and they need a new quote. This prevents the company from taking FX risk on stale rates.
pitfalls: Never serve a rate without a TTL — rates change constantly
Lock rates at quote time, not at payment time — the gap could be minutes
Multiple providers can give wildly different rates — need aggregation logic
Rate spikes can cause huge losses — add circuit breakers and limits
prompts.hint: Airwallex makes money on FX spreads — they care deeply about rate accuracy, locking, and how you handle provider failures.

prompts.text: Design a KYC/AML compliance system for onboarding. How do you handle screening at scale?
patterns: The failure mode I'm most concerned about is...
structure: Requirements: verify identity, screen against sanctions lists, PEP check, ongoing monitoring, audit trail
Estimation: 5K onboardings/day, 500K ongoing monitoring checks/day, <2s identity verification
High-level: Onboarding API → Document Verification → Screening Service → Risk Scoring → Decision Engine → Audit Log

Deep dive: Document verification via third-party (Jumio/Onfido). Screening: fuzzy name matching against OFAC/EU/UN sanctions lists, stored in Elasticsearch for sub-second search. Risk scoring: ML model on entity features. Ongoing: event-driven — every transaction triggers a screening check against updated watchlists.
Failure modes: Third-party verification down → queue and process async; False positive on screening → manual review queue; Watchlist update lag → daily full rescreen of all customers
prompts.sample_answer: KYC/AML has two phases: onboarding and ongoing. At onboarding, the user uploads ID documents. We send these to a verification provider who checks authenticity and does liveness detection. In parallel, we screen the user's name and entity against sanctions lists. I'd store sanctions lists in Elasticsearch with phonetic matching (double metaphone) because names can be transliterated differently. If the screening hits, it goes to a manual review queue. A compliance officer reviews and either clears or rejects. For ongoing monitoring, I'd subscribe to watchlist updates via a feed, and whenever a list changes, I'd rescreen all affected customers. Each check is logged immutably for regulatory audits.
pitfalls: Name matching must be fuzzy — 'Abdul Rahman' vs 'Abd al-Rahman' must match
Screening results must never be deleted — regulators audit your decisions
False positives are expensive — every one requires manual review
Watchlists update daily — you need to rescreen, not just check new customers
prompts.hint: Fintech compliance is non-negotiable. Show you understand that every decision must be auditable and that screening is ongoing, not one-time.

prompts.text: Design an API gateway for fintech services. How do you handle auth, rate limiting, and routing?
structure: Requirements: authenticate API keys/OAuth, rate limit per client, route to microservices, request/response transformation
Estimation: 50K RPS, 200 API clients, 15 backend services
High-level: Client → API Gateway → Auth Service → Rate Limiter → Router → Backend Services

Deep dive: Auth: validate JWT tokens or API keys via Redis lookup. Rate limiting: token bucket per client+endpoint in Redis. Routing: path-based to service discovery (Consul/K8s). Transformation: protocol translation (REST to gRPC). Circuit breaker on each backend route.
Failure modes: Auth service down → cache valid tokens for 5 min grace period; Redis down → local rate limit as fallback; Backend overloaded → circuit breaker returns 503 with retry-after header
prompts.sample_answer: For a fintech API gateway, security and reliability are paramount. I'd implement authentication as a JWT validation step — each request carries a signed JWT with the client's ID, permissions, and rate limit tier. The gateway validates the signature and extracts claims without calling an auth service for every request, keeping latency low. For rate limiting, I'd use a Redis-backed token bucket per client per endpoint. Fintech clients have different tiers — a small merchant might get 100 req/min while a large enterprise gets 10K. The router uses service discovery to find healthy backend instances. I'd add circuit breakers per backend service — if the payment service starts failing, the gateway returns a 503 immediately instead of timing out. This prevents cascading failures.
pitfalls: Never store API keys in plaintext — hash them like passwords
Rate limits must be per-endpoint, not just per-client — different operations have different costs
Gateway must never become a bottleneck — keep it stateless, push state to Redis
JWT validation must check expiry, audience, and issuer — not just signature
prompts.hint: Airwallex has an open API platform. They care about: multi-tenant auth, per-client rate limits, and graceful degradation when backends fail.

prompts.text: Design a webhook notification system for payment status updates. How do you guarantee delivery?
patterns: The failure mode I'm most concerned about is...
structure: Requirements: notify merchants of payment status changes, at-least-once delivery, ordered per payment, retry with backoff
Estimation: 1M webhooks/day, 99.9% delivery within 30s, average 3 retries per webhook
High-level: Event Source → Event Bus (Kafka) → Webhook Dispatcher → HTTP POST → Retry Queue → Dead Letter Queue

Deep dive: Each payment state change publishes to Kafka. Dispatcher consumes, looks up merchant's webhook URL, sends HTTP POST with HMAC signature. If response is not 2xx, retry with exponential backoff (1s, 5s, 30s, 5min, 30min). After 5 failures, move to DLQ for manual inspection. Ordering: partition by payment_id so events for the same payment are delivered in order.
Failure modes: Merchant endpoint down → retry queue absorbs; Kafka lag → scale consumers; Webhook flood → rate limit per merchant; HMAC mismatch on merchant side → they can verify integrity
prompts.sample_answer: Webhooks are critical for fintech — merchants need to know when a payment succeeds or fails. I'd design this as an event-driven system. When a payment changes state, the payment service publishes an event to Kafka. The webhook dispatcher consumes the event, looks up the merchant's registered webhook URL, and sends an HTTP POST with a JSON payload and an HMAC-SHA256 signature header. The merchant can verify the signature to confirm it's from us. If the POST fails or returns a non-2xx status, I retry with exponential backoff. I'd partition Kafka by payment_id so that events for the same payment are always processed in order — merchants expect to see 'authorized' before 'captured', not the other way around. After 5 failed retries, the event goes to a dead letter queue that the ops team monitors.
pitfalls: Always sign webhooks with HMAC — merchants must verify authenticity
Ordering matters — payment.captured must come after payment.authorized
Don't block the main flow on webhook delivery — it must be async
Idempotency on the merchant side is their responsibility, but you should document it
prompts.hint: Airwallex webhooks carry sensitive financial data. They care about: guaranteed delivery, ordering, security (HMAC), and how you handle merchant outages.

prompts.text: Design a bank connectivity layer that integrates with multiple banking partners. How do you handle different protocols and failures?
patterns: The first thing I would do is...
structure: Requirements: connect to 50+ banks via different protocols (SWIFT, SEPA, Faster Payments, local rails), abstract protocol differences, handle bank-specific quirks
Estimation: 10K payments/day per bank, 99.5% per-bank availability (banks are unreliable), sub-5s submission
High-level: Payment Service → Bank Router → Adapter Layer → Bank-Specific Connectors → Banks

Deep dive: Adapter pattern — one adapter per bank or protocol. Each adapter translates our standard payment model to the bank's format (ISO 20022, SWIFT MT, proprietary XML). Bank router selects the best rail based on cost, speed, and beneficiary country. Circuit breaker per bank — if Bank A is failing, route to Bank B.

Failure modes: Bank timeout → retry with idempotency key; Bank rejects payment → map error code to our error taxonomy; Bank offline → circuit breaker opens, queue payments for later; Adapter bug → canary deployment per adapter
prompts.sample_answer: The bank connectivity layer is the messiest part of any fintech system because every bank is different. I'd use the adapter pattern: define a standard internal payment interface, and implement one adapter per bank or protocol. The adapter translates our internal model to the bank's format — whether that's SWIFT MT103 for international wires, SEPA XML for Euro payments, or a proprietary REST API for a local bank. The bank router sits in front and decides which bank to use based on the payment's currency, amount, and destination country. It considers cost (some rails are cheaper), speed (Faster Payments is instant, SWIFT takes days), and reliability. Each bank adapter has its own circuit breaker — if HSBC starts timing out, we route Citi instead for USD payments. All submissions carry our internal idempotency key so we never double-send a payment.
pitfalls: Never assume banks follow their own spec — always have a human-debuggable log of raw messages
Bank availability is much worse than cloud services — design for 99.5%, not 99.99%
Error codes are not standardized — map every bank's error to your own taxonomy
Some banks have daily cutoff times — queue payments that miss the cutoff
prompts.hint: This is a uniquely fintech problem. Airwallex connects to 50+ banking partners. Show you understand: adapter pattern, circuit breaker per bank, and the reality that banks are unreliable.

patterns.name: Arrays & Hashing
patterns.description: The foundation of all coding interview problems. Arrays give O(1) access by index, hash maps give O(1) access by key.
patterns.interview_tip: When you hear "find a pair" or "check if exists", reach for a hash map first. It turns O(n²) into O(n).
common_pitfalls: Using array scan instead of hash map
Not handling duplicates
Forgetting about space complexity

patterns.name: Two Pointers
patterns.description: Use two pointers moving through an array to find pairs, triplets, or detect patterns. Converging pointers for sorted arrays, fast/slow for cycles.
patterns.interview_tip: If the array is sorted, two pointers can often replace a hash map and save space.
common_pitfalls: Forgetting to sort first for converging pointers
Off-by-one in while left < right
Not skipping duplicates in 3Sum

patterns.name: Stack
patterns.description: Last-in-first-out data structure. The go-to for parsing nested structures, tracking recent state, and monotonic problems.
patterns.interview_tip: When the problem involves "matching pairs" or "recent history", think stack.
common_pitfalls: Not checking empty stack before pop/peek
Using stack when a counter would suffice
Forgetting monotonic stack pattern

patterns.name: Binary Search
patterns.description: Divide and conquer on sorted data. O(log n) search by halving the search space each iteration.
patterns.interview_tip: Binary search is not just for searching — use it when the answer space is ordered and you can test a candidate in O(n).
common_pitfalls: mid = (left + right) // 2 can overflow in some languages
Off-by-one in boundary updates
Infinite loop when left = mid

patterns.name: Linked Lists
patterns.description: Linear data structure where each node points to the next. Tests pointer manipulation and edge case handling.
patterns.interview_tip: Always use a dummy head node. It eliminates edge cases when the head itself might change.
common_pitfalls: Not handling empty list
Losing reference to next node during reversal
Forgetting to update tail.next to null

patterns.name: Sliding Window
patterns.description: Maintain a window over contiguous elements. Slide it forward by adding one element and removing another. Turns O(n²) substring problems into O(n).
patterns.interview_tip: Keywords like "subarray", "substring", "contiguous" are sliding window signals.
common_pitfalls: Not handling variable window size correctly
Forgetting to update window state when shrinking
Using sliding window when prefix sum suffices

patterns.name: Trees
patterns.description: Hierarchical data structure. Most tree problems are solved recursively — process the current node, then recurse on children.
patterns.interview_tip: 90% of tree problems are either DFS recursion or BFS with a queue. Identify which one first.
common_pitfalls: Not handling null nodes
Confusing BST properties
Using recursion when iterative is clearer for BFS

patterns.name: Graphs
patterns.description: Nodes connected by edges. Most graph problems involve traversal (BFS/DFS), connectivity (components), or ordering (topological sort).
patterns.interview_tip: Always clarify: directed or undirected? Weighted or unweighted? Connected or disconnected? These change the approach.
common_pitfalls: Not tracking visited nodes (infinite loop)
Using DFS when BFS gives shorter path
Building adjacency matrix when list suffices

patterns.name: Dynamic Programming
patterns.description: Break problems into overlapping subproblems. Cache results to avoid recomputation. The key is finding the state transition equation.
patterns.interview_tip: Start with the recursive brute force. Then add memoization. Then convert to bottom-up if needed. Show your thought process.
common_pitfalls: Jumping straight to bottom-up DP
Wrong base cases
Not recognizing when greedy works instead of DP

patterns.name: Heaps & Priority Queues
patterns.description: Tree-based structure giving O(log n) insert and remove-min/max. Solves k-th element, merging sorted sequences, and running statistics.
patterns.interview_tip: Python's heapq is a min-heap. For max-heap, push negative values. For custom objects, push (priority, item) tuples.
common_pitfalls: Not knowing heapq is min-heap by default
Forgetting that heapify is O(n) not O(n log n)
Using sort when you need repeated min/max access

patterns.name: Intervals
patterns.description: Problems involving ranges, meetings, or time windows. The key pattern: sort by start time, then merge or compare.
patterns.interview_tip: Always sort intervals by start time first. After sorting, most interval problems become linear scans.
common_pitfalls: Forgetting to sort first
Not handling edge cases (empty, single interval)
Confusing merge vs insert logic
